covered_sections: End of unit questions and problems

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Differences Between Electric Field and Magnetic Field
questions.structural_anchor: Differences Between Electric Field and Magnetic Field
questions.question: A proton moves through a region of space. Under what condition will it experience a magnetic force?

options.B: When it moves parallel to the magnetic field
options.C: When it moves perpendicular to the magnetic field
options.D: When it moves in any direction relative to the field

questions.explanation: Magnetic force is given by \(\vec{F} = q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}\), which is zero when \(\vec{v}\) is parallel to \(\vec{B}\) (cross product zero). Maximum when perpendicular. Option A gives zero force.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Differences Between Electric Field and Magnetic Field
source_trace.structural_element: Differences Between Electric Field and Magnetic Field
source_trace.content_anchor: magnetic field caused by a dipole of the magnet

explanations_tiered.definition: The magnetic force on a moving charge is given by \(\vec{F} = q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}\), perpendicular to both velocity and field.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The cross product is zero when velocity is parallel or antiparallel to the magnetic field, maximum when perpendicular.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A charged particle feels a magnetic force only when it moves across the magnetic field lines, not along them.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \(\vec{F} = q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}\), the magnitude is \(F = |q|vB\sin\theta\). When \(\theta = 90^\circ\) (perpendicular), \(\sin\theta = 1\) (maximum). When \(\theta = 0^\circ\) (parallel), force is zero.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Lorentz force law combines electric and magnetic forces: \(\vec{F} = q(\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B})\). The magnetic component does no work as it is always perpendicular to displacement.
reasoning_steps: Write the Lorentz force law for magnetic component.
Identify that cross product depends on \(\sin\theta\).
\(\sin\theta = 0\) when parallel, \(\sin\theta = 1\) when perpendicular.

formula_analysis.name: Magnetic force on a moving charge

misconceptions: Some think magnetic force acts on any moving charge regardless of direction; it only acts when velocity has a component perpendicular to B.
Stationary charges experience no magnetic force, only electric force.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: \(F_B = qvB\sin\theta\); zero when \(\vec{v} \parallel \vec{B}\), max when perpendicular.

glossary_entry.definition: A charged particle experiences magnetic force only when its velocity has a component perpendicular to the magnetic field.

flashcard.front: When does a moving charge experience maximum magnetic force?
flashcard.back: When it moves perpendicular to the magnetic field (\(\theta = 90^\circ\)).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Condition for maximum magnetic force on a moving charge?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Magnetic force is zero when velocity is parallel to B, maximum when perpendicular.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: F = qvB sinθ, max at θ=90°.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use right-hand rule to demonstrate direction and dependence on angle.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the difference between electric and magnetic forces on charges
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Magnetic force \(F = qvB\sin\theta\) is maximum when \(\sin\theta = 1\) (\(\theta = 90^\circ\), perpendicular).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Iron filings sprinkled around a bar magnet align themselves along the magnetic field lines. What does the density of these filings indicate?

options.A: The direction of the magnetic field
options.B: The strength (magnitude) of the magnetic field

questions.explanation: The density of magnetic field lines (and thus iron filings) is proportional to the magnetic field strength. More lines per unit area indicates a stronger B field.

source_trace.content_anchor: The strength of the field is proportional to the closeness of the lines

explanations_tiered.definition: The density of magnetic field lines (number per unit area) indicates the magnitude of the magnetic field strength.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Closer field lines mean stronger magnetic field. Iron filings align and concentrate where the field is stronger, such as near the poles.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Where the iron filings are thick and close together, the magnet is strongest — like near the ends of a bar magnet.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Magnetic field strength B is proportional to the number of field lines crossing a unit area perpendicular to B. Denser lines → larger B.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In the limit, \(B = d\Phi/dA_{\perp}\), so flux density is exactly the number of field lines per unit area when drawn consistently.
reasoning_steps: Observe iron filing pattern around magnet.
Identify regions with highest filing density (near poles).
Recall that field strength is greatest where lines are closest.
Conclude density indicates field strength.
misconceptions: Density indicates direction? No, direction is given by the tangent, not density.
Denser lines do not indicate temperature or polarity.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Closer field lines = stronger magnetic field.

glossary_entry.definition: The number of magnetic field lines per unit area, proportional to the magnetic field strength.

flashcard.front: What does the density of iron filings around a magnet indicate?
flashcard.back: The strength (magnitude) of the magnetic field.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does dense iron filings near a magnet pole indicate?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Density of field lines (iron filings) shows relative magnetic field magnitude.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show actual iron filing patterns for bar magnets.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe properties of magnetic field lines
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The density of iron filings (field lines) is proportional to magnetic field strength.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A loop of wire is placed in a uniform magnetic field. The magnetic flux through the loop is maximum when the angle between the magnetic field and the normal to the loop is:
questions.explanation: Flux \(\Phi = BA\cos\theta\). \(\cos\theta\) is maximum at \(\theta = 0^\circ\) (field perpendicular to loop, normal aligned with field). At \(90^\circ\), flux is zero.

explanations_tiered.definition: Magnetic flux is given by \(\Phi = BA\cos\theta\), where \(\theta\) is the angle between \(\vec{B}\) and the area normal.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Flux is maximum when the field is perpendicular to the loop (\(\theta = 0^\circ\)), because all field lines pass through the area.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You get the most magnetic field through a hoop when the field is pointing straight through it, not sideways.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: At \(\theta = 0^\circ\), \(\cos\theta = 1\), so \(\Phi = BA\). As \(\theta\) increases, \(\cos\theta\) decreases, reducing flux.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The dot product \(\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A} = BA\cos\theta\) is maximum when \(\vec{B}\) and \(\vec{A}\) are parallel (\(\theta = 0\)).
reasoning_steps: Write flux formula: \(\Phi = BA\cos\theta\).
Identify that \(\cos\theta\) ranges from 0 to 1.
Maximum when \(\cos\theta = 1\) → \(\theta = 0^\circ\).
misconceptions: Some think flux is maximum when field is parallel to the plane (\(\theta = 90^\circ\)), but that gives zero flux.
Confusing angle measured from loop plane vs. normal.

glossary_entry.definition: Occurs when the magnetic field is perpendicular to the loop area, i.e., \(\theta = 0^\circ\).

flashcard.front: For maximum magnetic flux through a loop, what angle between B and normal?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: When is magnetic flux through a loop maximum?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Flux is maximum when the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of the loop.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a hoop and a stick to show perpendicular vs parallel orientation.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. \(\Phi = BA\cos\theta\) is maximum when \(\cos\theta = 1\) → \(\theta = 0^\circ\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction and Lenz's Law
questions.structural_anchor: Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction and Lenz's Law
questions.question: According to Lenz's law, the direction of the induced current is such that its magnetic field opposes the change in magnetic flux. This is a direct consequence of which conservation principle?
questions.hint: Think about what would happen if induced current aided the change.
questions.explanation: Lenz's law ensures that work must be done to change magnetic flux, converting mechanical energy to electrical energy. Without opposition, energy would not be conserved.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction and Lenz's Law
source_trace.structural_element: Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction and Lenz's Law
source_trace.content_anchor: Lenz's law depends on the principle of conservation of energy

explanations_tiered.definition: Lenz's law is a manifestation of conservation of energy in electromagnetic induction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If the induced current aided the change, it would amplify itself without external work, creating energy from nothing.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You have to do work to move a magnet near a coil; that work turns into electricity. If Lenz's law didn't oppose, you'd get energy for free.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Lenz's law ensures that the induced current's magnetic field opposes the change, requiring external work to overcome the opposition. This work is converted to electrical energy.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From Faraday's law, \(\epsilon = -d\Phi/dt\). The negative sign (Lenz's law) ensures that \(\vec{F} \cdot d\vec{x}\) is positive for work done by external agent, satisfying energy conservation.
reasoning_steps: Lenz: induced current opposes the flux change.
Opposition means external work is required to cause the change.
That work converts to electrical energy.
If opposition were not present, energy would be created (violates conservation).
misconceptions: Lenz's law is often confused with Newton's third law, but it is specifically tied to energy conservation.
Conservation of charge and mass are not directly related to induction direction.

glossary_entry.definition: The law that induced current opposes the flux change, ensuring conservation of energy.

flashcard.front: Lenz's law is a consequence of which conservation law?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What conservation law underlies Lenz's law?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Lenz's law ensures that energy is conserved in electromagnetic induction processes.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss what would happen if induced current aided the change (perpetual motion).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: State Lenz's law and its significance
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Lenz's law is a consequence of conservation of energy; opposition ensures work must be done.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Working principle of transformer in house appliances
questions.structural_anchor: Working principle of transformer in house appliances
questions.question: A student argues that a transformer can work with direct current (DC) because a changing current is not needed — the magnetic field from steady DC should still induce voltage. Is this correct?

options.A: Yes, because DC also produces a magnetic field
options.B: No, because DC produces a constant magnetic field, which does not induce emf
options.C: Yes, but only if the DC voltage is very high
options.D: No, because transformers require AC for the core to magnetize

questions.hint: Recall Faraday's law: induced emf requires changing flux.
questions.explanation: A steady DC current produces a constant magnetic field, not a changing one. Faraday's law requires a changing flux to induce emf, so a transformer does not work with steady DC.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Working principle of transformer in house appliances
source_trace.structural_element: Working principle of transformer in house appliances
source_trace.content_anchor: transformer operates on AC, not DC

explanations_tiered.definition: Transformers operate on electromagnetic induction, which requires a changing magnetic flux, produced by alternating current (AC).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Steady DC produces constant flux; constant flux induces zero emf. Only changing current (AC) induces voltage in the secondary.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A transformer needs a changing magnetic field to work, like a wave. DC is flat, not a wave — no induction.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Faraday's law: \(\epsilon \propto d\Phi/dt\). DC gives \(d\Phi/dt = 0\), so \(\epsilon = 0\). AC gives \(d\Phi/dt \neq 0\), inducing emf.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In the primary, \(V_P = L_P dI_P/dt\). For DC, \(dI_P/dt = 0\) after transients, so \(V_P = 0\) across the primary (ideal case), and no flux change in secondary.
reasoning_steps: Transformer operation based on Faraday's law.
Induced emf requires changing magnetic flux.
DC current produces constant magnetic field.
Constant flux → no induced emf → transformer fails.
misconceptions: Some think any magnetic field induces emf, but it must be changing.
A transformer connected to DC may briefly induce a pulse when switched on/off (transient), but not continuous operation.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Transformer needs AC (changing flux), not steady DC.

glossary_entry.definition: Transformers do not operate on steady DC because a constant magnetic field does not induce an emf.

flashcard.front: Can a transformer work with steady DC current?
flashcard.back: No, because DC produces constant flux, and induced emf requires changing flux (Faraday's law).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why doesn't a transformer work with steady DC?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: DC produces constant magnetic flux, and Faraday's law requires changing flux to induce emf.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Transformers require alternating current to produce the changing magnetic flux necessary for induction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with a simple transformer and DC source showing no secondary voltage after initial transient.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the working principle of a transformer
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Steady DC gives constant flux; induced emf requires changing flux (Faraday's law).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A graphics tablet (digitizer) uses electromagnetic induction to detect the position of a special pen. What component in the pen creates the changing magnetic field?

options.B: A tiny electromagnet or resonant coil

questions.hint: The pen generates its own magnetic field.
questions.explanation: The pen contains a small coil that resonates at a specific frequency, producing an oscillating magnetic field. The tablet's grid of wires detects the induced currents to locate the pen.

source_trace.content_anchor: pen has a tiny magnetic field coming from the tip

explanations_tiered.definition: Graphics tablet pens contain a resonant coil that produces an oscillating magnetic field, detected by the tablet's sensor grid.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The changing magnetic field from the pen induces currents in the tablet's wire grid, allowing position tracking via electromagnetic induction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The pen sends out a tiny magnetic signal; the tablet feels that signal and knows exactly where the pen is.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The pen's coil resonates at a frequency; the tablet emits a signal to excite it, then detects the pen's induced field in its grid wires.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This is an example of mutual inductance: the tablet's grid and pen's coil form a coupled inductor system; position is triangulated from signal strengths in different grid lines.
reasoning_steps: Pen contains coil.
Coil produces oscillating magnetic field when energized.
Tablet grid detects induced currents.
Position calculated from signal distribution.
misconceptions: The pen does not contain a permanent magnet; it uses an electromagnet or resonant circuit.
It is not pressure-based; pressure may be separate but position is magnetic.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Graphics tablet pen: resonant coil → oscillating B-field → induction in grid.

glossary_entry.definition: A stylus containing a resonant coil that produces an oscillating magnetic field for position tracking via electromagnetic induction.

flashcard.front: What does a graphics tablet pen use to create a magnetic field?
flashcard.back: A tiny electromagnet or resonant coil.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does a graphics tablet pen create a magnetic field?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A tiny resonant coil (electromagnet) produces an oscillating magnetic field.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A small coil in the pen that resonates to produce an alternating magnetic field for induction-based position sensing.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to wireless charging and RFID as similar induction applications.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe applications of electromagnetism in daily life
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The pen contains a resonant coil that produces an oscillating (changing) magnetic field, detected by the tablet's grid.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a reed relay, what causes the two nickel-iron blades to make electrical contact?

options.B: A magnetic field aligning their magnetic poles oppositely

questions.hint: The blades become magnets with opposite polarities.
questions.explanation: When current flows through the coil around the reed relay, it creates a magnetic field. This field magnetizes the blades, giving them opposite magnetic poles (one north, one south), which attract and close the contact.

source_trace.content_anchor: field induced by a current... causes the two blades to assume opposite magnetic polarities, thereby forcing them to attract together

explanations_tiered.definition: A reed relay uses an electromagnet to induce opposite magnetic poles in two ferromagnetic blades, causing them to attract and close the circuit.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The coil's magnetic field magnetizes the blades. Opposite poles attract, bringing the blades together to make electrical contact.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Electricity in the coil turns the two metal strips into magnets with opposite ends, so they snap together like a magnet and fridge.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The magnetic field from the coil induces a north pole on one blade and a south pole on the other. Opposite poles attract, closing the switch.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The blades are made of a soft ferromagnetic material (nickel-iron) with low coercivity, allowing them to be magnetized and demagnetized easily. The attractive force \(F \propto (M_1 M_2)/r^2\) closes the gap.
reasoning_steps: Current in coil creates magnetic field.
Field magnetizes both blades.
Induced poles are opposite (N on one, S on other).
Opposite poles attract → blades contact.
misconceptions: Heating is not the mechanism; it's magnetic attraction.
The coil does not move mechanically; the blades move due to magnetism.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Reed relay: coil magnetizes blades with opposite poles → attraction → switch closes.

glossary_entry.definition: A magnetic field induces opposite magnetic poles in two blades, causing them to attract and complete an electrical circuit.

flashcard.front: How does a reed relay close its contacts?
flashcard.back: Magnetic field induces opposite poles in blades, causing magnetic attraction.

visual_system.visual_description: A coil around a glass envelope containing two nickel-iron blades. Magnetic field lines show opposite poles induced on blades.
visual_system.caption: Coil current creates field; blades become opposite poles and attract.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Diagram: Coil wrapped around glass tube with two overlapping metal blades inside. Magnetic field lines from coil cause blades to have opposite poles (N on left blade, S on right blade), attracting them together.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What makes reed relay blades close?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Magnetic field induces opposite poles, causing magnetic attraction.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Opposite magnetic poles induced in blades attract to complete the circuit.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show a real reed relay and how a magnet can also activate it.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the working principle of a magnetic relay
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The induced magnetic field gives the blades opposite polarities, causing attraction and contact.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What role do the brushes play in a DC electric motor?

options.A: They increase the magnetic field strength
options.B: They conduct current between stationary wires and the rotating commutator
options.C: They provide mechanical support for the coil
options.D: They reverse the polarity of the permanent magnets

questions.hint: Brushes maintain electrical connection to the rotating part.
questions.explanation: Brushes are stationary carbon or metal contacts that press against the rotating commutator, allowing current to flow from the stationary power supply into the rotating coil.

source_trace.content_anchor: brushes conduct current between stationary wires and moving parts

explanations_tiered.definition: Brushes are stationary conductive contacts that slide against the rotating commutator to supply current to the motor's coil.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: As the commutator rotates, brushes maintain electrical continuity, delivering current to the appropriate coil segments at the right time.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Brushes are like the wires that touch the spinning part so electricity can keep flowing into the coil.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Brushes are typically made of carbon or graphite. They press against the commutator segments, transferring current from the stationary external circuit to the rotating coil.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Brushes must have low friction and good conductivity. The brush-commutator interface causes voltage drop and wear, a limitation of brushed DC motors.
reasoning_steps: Power supply is stationary.
Coil rotates with commutator.
Brushes provide sliding electrical contact.
Current flows from supply → brushes → commutator → coil.
misconceptions: Brushes do not strengthen the magnetic field; that is the core or stator magnets.
They do not reverse magnet polarity; the commutator reverses current direction.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Brushes: stationary contacts to rotating commutator.

glossary_entry.definition: Stationary conductive elements that maintain electrical connection to the rotating commutator in a DC motor.

flashcard.front: What is the function of brushes in a DC motor?
flashcard.back: Conduct current between stationary wires and the rotating commutator.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What do brushes do in a DC motor?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Conduct current between stationary wires and the rotating commutator.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Stationary contacts that supply current to the rotating coil via the commutator.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show a disassembled DC motor to identify brushes and commutator.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the components of a DC electric motor
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Brushes conduct current from the stationary power supply to the rotating commutator and coil.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A coil rotates at constant angular speed \(\omega\) in a uniform magnetic field \(B\). The graph of induced emf versus time is a sine wave. What physical quantity determines the amplitude (peak voltage) of this sine wave?

options.A: The resistance of the coil only

questions.explanation: Peak induced emf in a rotating coil is \(\epsilon_0 = NAB\omega\), where \(N\) is number of turns, \(A\) area, \(B\) field strength, and \(\omega\) angular speed.

explanations_tiered.definition: The peak emf in an AC generator is given by \(\epsilon_0 = NAB\omega\), where all four factors contribute.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Flux \(\Phi = NBA\cos(\omega t)\); emf \(\epsilon = -d\Phi/dt = NAB\omega \sin(\omega t)\). The amplitude is \(NAB\omega\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: To get more voltage from a generator, you can use a stronger magnet (B), spin faster (ω), use more wire loops (N), or make the coil bigger (A).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From Faraday's law: \(\epsilon = NAB\omega \sin(\omega t)\). The amplitude depends on all four: turns, area, field, and angular speed.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derivation: \(\Phi(t) = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}(t) = BA\cos\theta(t)\) with \(\theta = \omega t\). Then \(\epsilon = -d\Phi/dt = BA\omega \sin(\omega t)\). For N turns, multiply by N.
reasoning_steps: Write flux as function of time: \(\Phi = NBA\cos(\omega t)\).
Differentiate: \(\epsilon = -d\Phi/dt = NAB\omega \sin(\omega t)\).
Amplitude is the coefficient: \(\epsilon_0 = NAB\omega\).
misconceptions: Frequency alone does not determine amplitude; amplitude depends on ω times NAB.
Resistance affects current, not induced emf amplitude.

glossary_entry.definition: The maximum induced voltage in an AC generator, equal to \(NAB\omega\).

flashcard.front: What factors determine peak emf in an AC generator?
flashcard.back: \(\epsilon_0 = NAB\omega\): turns, area, magnetic field, angular speed.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Peak emf in AC generator formula?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Maximum induced voltage in a rotating-coil generator, given by \(NAB\omega\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Derive from \(\Phi = BA\cos(\omega t)\) and differentiate.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the working principle of an AC generator
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \(\epsilon_0 = NAB\omega\). All four factors (turns, area, field, angular speed) determine amplitude.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the relationship between the direction of a magnetic field and the tangent to a magnetic field line at any point?
questions.hint: Recall the definition of a field line.
questions.explanation: By definition, the tangent to a magnetic field line at any point gives the direction of the magnetic field vector \(\vec{B}\) at that point. Thus they are parallel.

source_trace.content_anchor: direction of the magnetic field is tangent to the field line at any point in space

explanations_tiered.definition: A magnetic field line is defined such that its tangent at any point points in the direction of the magnetic field at that point.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Field lines are drawn to visualize the field; the tangent gives the instantaneous direction of \(\vec{B}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Magnetic field lines are like arrows showing which way a compass points. The line direction and the compass direction match.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: By construction, \(\vec{B}\) is tangent to the field line at every point. This is the fundamental definition of a field line.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From \(d\vec{l} \times \vec{B} = 0\) along a field line, the line element \(d\vec{l}\) is parallel to \(\vec{B}\), so the tangent direction equals \(\vec{B}\) direction.
reasoning_steps: Recall field line definition: line whose tangent = field direction.
Therefore tangent and field are parallel (same direction).
misconceptions: Perpendicular: some confuse with electric field lines and equipotentials.
Opposite: the tangent is never opposite; it points exactly along \(\vec{B}\).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Tangent to field line = direction of \(\vec{B}\).

glossary_entry.definition: The line that touches a magnetic field line at a point, pointing in the direction of the magnetic field at that point.

flashcard.front: What is the relationship between B direction and field line tangent?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Direction of B field relative to field line tangent?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The tangent to a magnetic field line gives the magnetic field direction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw field lines and tangent arrows to illustrate.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe properties of magnetic field lines
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The tangent to a magnetic field line at any point gives the direction of \(\vec{B}\).
